_meta._file_role: v14 entry-data fragment. Designed for union-merge into Map_v12_data.json (the v13 data file) to produce a v14-complete data set. nodes and edges arrays contain only the additions; families and glossary are unchanged from v13.

nodes.label: Topological phases
nodes.full_name: Classification of topological phases of matter
nodes.description: Classification of topological phases of gapped quantum matter. The free-fermion case is largely solved by the ten-fold way (Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev) and its crystalline-symmetry extensions, which give complete K-theory classifications across dimensions. The broader problem — interacting topological phases, fractional / symmetry-enriched / symmetry-protected topological order in strongly-correlated systems, and the classification of topological order with anyonic excitations — remains open. The structural reason is reach-termination: the K-theory machinery that closes the free-fermion case reaches its applicable limit at interactions, where the classification can collapse (e.g. ℤ → ℤ₈ for class BDI in 1d under interactions; Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010) or be enriched in ways the free-fermion apparatus cannot capture. New classification machinery (cobordism for invertible phases, fusion categories for topological order, generalized cohomology theories) is partially developed and partially overlapping but not yet closure-grade for the full interacting domain.
nodes.closure_condition: Awaiting: (i) classification machinery for interacting fermionic SPT phases that subsumes Fidkowski-Kitaev-type collapses; (ii) a complete classification of (2+1)d topological order beyond the modular tensor category framework's current reach; (iii) classification of symmetry-enriched topological order across symmetry groups and dimensions; (iv) integration of crystalline-symmetry, magnetic-symmetry, and internal-symmetry classification programs into a single framework.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Tensor-network / matrix-product-state methods provide classification machinery for (1+1)d SPT phases (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011 and subsequent work); 2d projected entangled pair state (PEPS) classification is partially developed. Discharge is partial — significant content classified but not closure-grade.

citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT phases)
Wen 2017, Rev. Mod. Phys. 89, 041004 (review of topological order)
Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005 (review of free-fermion classification)
nodes.placement_rationale: Within-architecture frontier hosted by QFT (specifically the condensed-matter / many-body QFT sub-domain). Stratum 4 placement reflects that the frontier is internal to QFT rather than cross-architecture. Added in v14 because no condensed-matter frontier existed in v13 and the ten-fold way needed a bears-on target.

nodes.label: Ten-fold way
nodes.full_name: Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev classification of topological phases of free fermions
nodes.description: Classification of gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians by their discrete symmetries (time-reversal T, particle-hole C, chiral S = T·C) into ten symmetry classes (the Altland-Zirnbauer ten), each of which carries a topological invariant — trivial (0), ℤ, ℤ₂, or 2ℤ — at each spatial dimension d, with a period-eight (real classes) and period-two (complex classes) Bott pattern across dimensions. The structure was identified for random-matrix ensembles by Altland and Zirnbauer (1997), extended to topological phases in 3d by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008), and completed across all dimensions with the Bott-clock structure made explicit by Kitaev (2009). The 'periodic table' framing is Kitaev's.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Gapped Hamiltonians of free (non-interacting) fermions in any spatial dimension d ≥ 0, with the three discrete symmetries T (anti-unitary, T² = ±1 or absent), C (anti-unitary particle-hole, C² = ±1 or absent), and S = T·C (unitary chiral, present iff both T and C present, or absent). Covers bulk topological invariants for crystalline systems in the absence of additional crystalline symmetries (with crystalline-symmetry extensions developed as a separate classification program). Excludes: interacting topological phases (where the classification can collapse or be enriched), fractional / symmetry-enriched topological order, topological order with anyonic excitations, gapless topological phases.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-class
classification_axes.description: The ten Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes, labeled by Cartan-class symbols. The class is determined by the triple (T², C², S-presence): A = (0,0,0); AIII = (0,0,1); AI = (+1,0,0); BDI = (+1,+1,1); D = (0,+1,0); DIII = (-1,+1,1); AII = (-1,0,0); CII = (-1,-1,1); C = (0,-1,0); CI = (+1,-1,1). Values listed in the canonical Bott-clock order used by Kitaev 2009 Table 1: complex classes (A, AIII) first, then the eight real classes in Bott-clock order starting from AI.

classification_axes.name: spatial-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spatial dimension of the gapped bulk. The classification extends to all d ≥ 0 with Bott-periodicity (period 8 for real classes, period 2 for complex classes); cells in this entry are populated for d ∈ {1, 2, 3} as the physically most-studied slice. Higher-dimensional content can be added without schema change.

cells.description: Class A in 1d: no nontrivial topological invariant.
Class A in 2d: Chern-number-classified phases. The integer quantum Hall effect is the canonical realization.
realized_examples: integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing et al. 1980)
anomalous quantum Hall effect in magnetically-doped (Bi,Sb)₂Te₃ thin films (Chang et al. 2013)

cells.description: Class A in 3d: trivial. The Bott pattern for complex classes alternates ℤ–0 with period 2.
Class AIII (chiral unitary) in 1d: ℤ-classified by a winding number.
realized_examples: Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model (Su-Schrieffer-Heeger 1979) — theoretical paradigm; physical realizations in cold-atom and photonic lattices

cells.description: Class AIII in 3d: ℤ-classified by a 3d winding number.
Class AI (T² = +1, no particle-hole, no chiral) is trivial in d = 1, 2, 3. The first nontrivial entry occurs at d = 4 (ℤ).

Class BDI (T² = +1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ-classified at the free-fermion level. The Kitaev chain (Kitaev 2001) is the canonical realization, with the winding number counting Majorana edge modes. Note: under interactions this classification collapses to ℤ₈ (Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010), a key example of the free-to-interacting reach-termination that motivates the open frontier.

Class D (particle-hole, C² = +1, no time-reversal) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified, with the nontrivial sector hosting unpaired Majorana zero modes at boundaries. Predicted by Kitaev 2001 (as the spinless p-wave case of his model). Experimental claims of Majorana zero modes in semiconductor-superconductor heterostructures (Mourik et al. 2012 and follow-ups) have undergone significant revision, with notable retractions; the cell-level classification is theoretically uncontested but experimental confirmation has tension.
realized_examples: semiconductor nanowire / superconductor heterostructures — disputed (see Mourik et al. 2012, Albrecht et al. 2016, Zhang et al. retraction 2021)
vortex cores in topological-superconductor candidates (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅; Wang et al. 2018, also disputed)

cells.description: Class D in 2d: ℤ-classified topological superconductors. Sr₂RuO₄ was a long-standing candidate; the chiral p-wave interpretation is now considered disfavored by polarized-neutron-scattering experiments (Pustogow et al. 2019).
Class DIII (T² = -1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor wires.
Class DIII in 3d: ℤ-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor. Superfluid ³He-B is the canonical realization.

Class AII (T² = -1, no particle-hole, no chiral) in 2d: ℤ₂-classified 'quantum spin Hall' topological insulators. The Kane-Mele model (2005) and BHZ model (Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006) predicted the phase; experimental confirmation came in HgTe/CdTe quantum wells (König et al. 2007).
realized_examples: InAs/GaSb double quantum wells (Knez et al. 2011)

cells.description: Class AII in 3d: ℤ₂-classified 3D topological insulators. Predicted independently by Fu-Kane-Mele (2007), Moore-Balents (2007), and Roy (2009); confirmed first in Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ (Hsieh et al. 2008) and Bi₂Se₃ / Bi₂Te₃ / Sb₂Te₃ (Xia et al. 2009, Chen et al. 2009).
realized_examples: Bi₂Se₃, Bi₂Te₃, Sb₂Te₃ family (Xia et al. 2009; Chen et al. 2009)

cells.description: Class CII (T² = -1, C² = -1, chiral) in 1d: 2ℤ-classified — the topological invariant takes even integer values.
Class C (particle-hole C² = -1, no time-reversal) in 2d: 2ℤ-classified. Singlet topological superconductors with broken time-reversal.
Class CI (T² = +1, C² = -1, chiral) in 3d: 2ℤ-classified time-reversal-invariant singlet topological superconductor.

closure_status.description: Complete within the stated domain (gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians, three discrete symmetries, no further crystalline symmetries). Closure was established by Kitaev (2009) via the K-theory / Bott-periodicity argument, confirming and extending the d = 3 cases worked out by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008). The 'within-domain' qualifier is load-bearing: closure does not extend to (a) interacting systems (where Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010 showed the BDI-d1 ℤ collapses to ℤ₈), (b) crystalline-symmetry-protected phases (a separate classification program), or (c) gapless topological phases.

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D quantum spin Hall insulator (class AII, d = 2, ℤ₂ classification) — a time-reversal-invariant insulator with helical edge states.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kane-Mele 2005, Phys. Rev. Lett. 95, 226801; Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 (HgTe-specific prediction)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D topological insulator (class AII, d = 3, ℤ₂ classification) — a class of bulk-insulating materials with topologically-protected surface states.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Fu-Kane-Mele 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 98, 106803; Moore-Balents 2007, Phys. Rev. B 75, 121306; Roy 2009, Phys. Rev. B 79, 195322
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Hsieh et al. 2008, Nature 452, 970 (Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ); Xia et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 398 (Bi₂Se₃)

predictive_yield.prediction: 1D topological superconductor wire (class D, d = 1, ℤ₂ classification) hosting Majorana zero modes at its endpoints.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Mourik et al. 2012, Science 336, 1003 (initial claim); Zhang et al. retraction 2021, Nature 591, 354 (retraction of a high-profile follow-up); current status reviewed in Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 350 and Yu et al. 2021, Nature Physics 17, 482
predictive_yield.description: The theoretical prediction is firm and the classification cell is uncontested. Experimental status has substantial tension: several high-profile claims of zero-bias-peak signatures consistent with Majorana zero modes have been challenged or retracted; the field has not yet converged on a confirmed material realization. Status 'unconfirmed-tension' rather than 'confirmed' or 'falsified' reflects this honestly.

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor (class DIII, d = 3, ℤ classification).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125; Qi-Hughes-Raghu-Zhang 2009, Phys. Rev. Lett. 102, 187001
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Realized in superfluid ³He-B (longstanding; topological reading consolidated in Volovik 2003, 'The Universe in a Helium Droplet')

predictive_yield.prediction: Integer quantum Hall effect (class A, d = 2, ℤ classification) — quantized Hall conductance at integer multiples of e²/h.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Discovered 1980 (von Klitzing-Dorda-Pepper, Phys. Rev. Lett. 45, 494); topological interpretation via TKNN invariant (Thouless-Kohmoto-Nightingale-den Nijs 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 49, 405); class A / ten-fold-way framing applied retrospectively.
predictive_yield.description: Recorded as retro-explanatory-only because the experimental phenomenon predates the ten-fold-way framework. The classification correctly hosts the IQHE; it did not predict it.

citations: Altland, Zirnbauer 1997, Phys. Rev. B 55, 1142 (the ten symmetry classes)
Schnyder, Ryu, Furusaki, Ludwig 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125 (classification of topological insulators and superconductors in 3d)
Kitaev 2009, AIP Conf. Proc. 1134, 22 (periodic table for topological insulators and superconductors; Bott-periodic structure across all dimensions)
Ryu, Schnyder, Furusaki, Ludwig 2010, New J. Phys. 12, 065010 (tenfold way and dimensional hierarchy; review and extension)
Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005 (comprehensive review of free-fermion topological classification including crystalline extensions)

edges.description: The ten-fold way fully solves the free-fermion sub-domain of the topological-phases-classification frontier. The remaining open content of the frontier — interacting phases, fractional / symmetry-enriched topological order, classification beyond K-theory — lies outside the ten-fold way's domain of applicability. The bears-on is partially-solves rather than constrains because within the free-fermion sub-domain the classification is genuinely closure-grade.
citations: Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (ℤ → ℤ₈ collapse for interacting BDI-d1, demarcating the ten-fold way's reach)
Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of interacting SPT phases beyond ten-fold-way reach)

edges.description: QFT (specifically the many-body / condensed-matter sub-domain) invokes the ten-fold way as the standard classification machinery for free-fermion topological phases. The classification is single-particle / quadratic-Hamiltonian level and uses QFT's BdG / single-particle formalism rather than QFT's interacting machinery; this is the same architectural reach-limit that the topological-phases-classification frontier records on the v13 side.
citations: Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005
